text: The video shows a direct defect in the right inguinal region, indicative of a direct inguinal hernia.
The surgeon carefully dissects and takes down the peritoneal flap laterally, exposing underlying structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon encounters adhesions at Bogros' space and carefully enters laterally to navigate through the area.
The surgeon extends the peritoneal flap medially beyond the symphysis pubis, focusing on addressing direct defects.
The surgeon approaches the deep internal ring, pulling down and away to reveal the anti-sac of a direct inguinal hernia.
The surgeon exposes the hernia sac, which begins to peek through a thin white lining, indicating the presence of the direct inguinal hernia.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to buzz in front of the hernia sac, applying downward traction to facilitate its separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses scissors without cautery to gently push up and away, peeling the tissue off from the surrounding structures.
The surgeon navigates towards the epigastric vessels, directing the instruments upwards and to the right, with the vessels becoming visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon manipulates the direct hernia by pulling the tissue down and away, revealing the hernia sac within the surgical field.
The muscle fibers are visibly splayed out, showing a clear separation and alignment within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a sweeping motion to push tissue up and away, applying additional cautery to safely separate the tissue layers.
The surgeon dissects tissue inferiorly below Cooper's ligament, extending the dissection past the pubic tubercle and down to the obturator fossa.
The surgeon works on the lateral plane, manipulating tissue to define the surgical field.
The surgeon maneuvers the tissue around the anatomical corner, continuing the dissection process with visible manipulation of the surrounding structures.
The surgeon performs a high ligation and carefully peels the sac down off the vas deferens, which is visible to the right of the scissors.
The surgeon applies traction and counter traction, causing the tissue planes to visibly separate and open up during the dissection.
The surgeon identifies two bridging veins of the coronamortis, visible in the surgical field.
The obturator fossa is visible just below the previously mentioned area, likely near the site of dissection.
The surgeon observes a significant amount of adipose tissue surrounding the iliac vessels.
The obturator nerve is visible as it is gently pulled out from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon positions the mesh medially to cover the direct defect before proceeding with lateral fixation.
The surgeon prepares to fixate the mesh in a triangular pattern over the direct hernia defect, ensuring optimal coverage and stability.
The surgeon places fixation points on Cooper's ligament and the pubic tubercle, followed by a fixation on the high medial portion of the mesh.
The surgeon closes the flap, completing the coverage over the surgical area.
